text:
ESL university students often spend many years at tertiary level learning English. Frequently teachers expect to see some significant progress in their students’ language use over this time but, unfortunately, few of them manage to break through to the next level of English proficiency. Research suggests that many have reached the B2 level in the ELSA Test of English and remain there, which is borne out by some results from the surveys referred to in this paper.

Reasons for Stagnation The authors highlight the constraints of current approaches to language teaching such as the Grammar-Translation Method and unthinking adherence to communicative language teaching (CLT) approaches which they suggest can perpetuate the phenomenon of the plateau. In order to succeed in facilitating students' progress, the writers suggest focusing on a third level of language learning which enhances students' overall confidence, creativity and willingness to take risks and therefore be more productive and dynamic in their use of language. Through the use of small group teaching and establishing a supportive learning environment the authors seek to draw students out of the comfort zone of repetitive, safe structures and motivate them to become more independent in their learning. Students' self-assessments highlighted lack of confidence as a major constraint to their learning and progress.
headline: Fully Human Written
